title: Coconut oil body polish
subtitle: A body scrub with coconut oil, sugar, and sweet orange essential oil
excerpt: A body polish made from virgin coconut oil and granulated sugar, scented with sweet orange essential oil. The coconut oil melts from body heat during application, leaving the skin oiled as the sugar exfoliates. Makes enough for 4 to 5 uses. Keep a dry spoon in the jar; water causes coconut oil to go rancid faster.
sourceNotes: Tisserand and Young, Essential Oil Safety (2nd ed., 2014): sweet orange (cold-pressed) at rinse-off rates. Formulators Kitchen body polish formulation (formulatorskitchen.com, accessed May 2026).

recipe.batchNotes: Double the batch for a larger jar or a gift.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The polish can be made weeks ahead. The coconut oil solidifies in cool weather and melts in warm weather; both states are usable.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 6 months from making. Virgin coconut oil is stable but can go rancid if water is repeatedly introduced into the jar. Discard if the polish smells sour, fermented, or waxy-rancid.

glossaryTerms.definition: Virgin coconut oil is solid at room temperatures below 24 degrees Celsius and liquid above that temperature. In a body polish, solid coconut oil provides a thick, scoopable consistency that melts from skin heat during application. In warm kitchens or in summer, the polish may become a looser, oilier texture; this is normal and it still works.

An occlusive ingredient forms a physical barrier on the skin surface that slows water loss. Coconut oil is a moderately occlusive carrier oil; applying it to damp skin after a shower traps a layer of moisture against the skin before it evaporates. The sugar in this polish exfoliates dead cells while the oil creates this barrier effect.

content.text: A body polish differs from a standard scrub in the oil-to-exfoliant ratio: more oil, with the oil acting as the main conditioner rather than just a binder. The
of coconut oil means the product changes character between winter and summer: it is scoopable and solid in winter, and looser in warm weather. Both textures work; the skin results are the same.
Apply to damp skin after a shower so the

oil can trap the moisture on the skin surface. The coconut oil makes the shower floor slippery; take care stepping out. Keep a dry spoon beside the jar; introducing water into the polish each time will shorten its shelf life significantly.

items.prepNote: Virgin coconut oil for a light coconut scent, or refined for a neutral base.
Standard granulated white sugar. The 1.5:1 sugar-to-oil ratio by weight gives a polish rather than a scrub texture.
2.3% of total product weight. Cold-pressed sweet orange peel oil. Complements the coconut base note.

content.text: Soften the coconut oil if needed

If the coconut oil is very firm (in cold weather), place the jar in a bowl of warm water for a few minutes until it softens to a spreadable consistency but is not liquid. The oil should be soft enough to stir easily.

Weigh the coconut oil, granulated sugar, and sweet orange essential oil into the mixing bowl. Stir until the sugar is evenly coated and the essential oil is distributed throughout. Spoon into a wide-mouthed glass jar and smooth the surface. Seal and label with the making date.

Store in a cool, dry place with the lid on. The polish keeps for 6 months when kept dry. Use a dry spoon for every scoop; wet hands introduce water and bacteria that will cause the coconut oil to go rancid much faster. Discard if the polish smells sour, fermented, or off in any way.
